topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed with an oppressive, buzzing monotony. Elias tapped his pen against the worn cover of his textbook, the rhythmic clicking a counterpoint to the frantic thumping in his chest. He reread the accusation, the words stinging like shards of glass in his mouth. *Plagiarism*. It felt like a physical blow, stealing the air from his lungs. He scrubbed a hand over his face, the skin clammy beneath his touch. Every glance from a passing student felt like a spotlight, dissecting his every move. He forced his gaze back to the screen, his fingers moving across the keyboard with a jerky, almost desperate speed, trying to reconstruct the argument from memory, a desperate scramble against the encroaching silence of impending judgment.

[story 2]

"Are you quite sure about this, Professor?" Maya asked, her voice pitched just low enough to avoid attracting undue attention, but carrying an edge of steel. Liam sat ramrod straight in the examination room, his knuckles white where he gripped the edge of the desk. He didn't look up immediately. Instead, he meticulously aligned the stack of notes before him, smoothing out any perceived wrinkles, a ritual born of sheer, unadulterated tension. Inside, a cold, sharp clarity cut through the anxiety. He reviewed the source material one last time, his mind racing through the nuances of the original text, searching for the exact phrasing he had inadvertently borrowed. A bead of sweat traced a cold path down his temple, and he blinked hard, forcing his vision to refocus on the blank page, refusing to let the doubt settle in his gut.

[story 3]

The air in the hallway felt thick, heavy, suffocating. Sarah walked with deliberate, measured steps, her spine unnaturally rigid. Each footfall seemed amplified in the quiet corridor, echoing the rapid drumbeat inside her own skull. She kept her hands clasped tightly behind her back, a self-imposed restraint against the urge to flee. When the notification arrived on her phone, the world seemed to slow down to a crawl, the colors blurring at the edges. She stood by the water fountain, the cool metal offering scant relief to the heat rising in her cheeks. She took a deep, slow breath, counting the seconds between inhaling and exhaling, gathering every scrap of inner composure she could muster before facing the inevitable confrontation.
